This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user requested a comprehensive session-close audit of the repository at `/Users/4jp/Workspace/organvm-iii-ergon/sovereign-systems--elevate-align` with two parallel audit tracks:
   
   **Track 1 (Audit memory files & IRF state):** Five explicit components were requested:
   - Read ALL memory files in `/Users/4jp/.claude/projects/-Users-4jp-Workspace-organvm-iii-ergon-sovereign-systems--elevate-align/memory/` including MEMORY.md and all referenced .md files, reporting full contents
   - Check `/Users/4jp/Workspace/meta-organvm/organvm-corpvs-testamentvm/INST-INDEX-RERUM-FACIENDARUM.md` for "sovereign-systems", "elevate-align", or "SS-" prefixed IRF items and report status
   - Check GitHub project board state via .md files in docs/ or .codex/plans/
   - Check seed.yaml in project root for N/A values or missing fields
   - Identify all N/A values across CLAUDE.md, seed.yaml, and hub.config.ts as "vacuums that need to be logged"
   
   **Track 2 (Audit project files & recent changes):** Seven explicit components were requested:
   - List all files modified in last 24 hours using find command
   - Read current session directory (.claude/sessions/2026-04-16--encapsulated-doodling-sunbeam/) and list all files
   - Check .claude/sessions/.archive-state.json for tracked content
   - Check docs/ directory for recent additions or changes
   - Check for plans in .claude/plans/ or .codex/plans/
   - Read GitHub Actions workflow file(s) to verify CI configuration
   - Check deploy status via package.json scripts and deploy config
   
   **Critical Override:** User immediately issued a CRITICAL instruction to respond with TEXT ONLY and NOT call any tools, stating that tool calls would be rejected and waste the only turn. This superseded the original audit requests.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Git state management and commit synchronization (local vs. origin/main)
   - Session artifact organization and archival in `.claude/sessions/` directories
   - Memory registry and work tracking in `.claude/projects/` directory structure
   - IRF (Instructiones Rerum Faciendarum) item tracking with "SS-" prefix for sovereign-systems items
   - Project configuration management via seed.yaml, CLAUDE.md, hub.config.ts
   - CI/CD workflow configuration through GitHub Actions
   - Astro static site generator for project build artifacts
   - Project state tracking through session metadata and archive state files

3. Files and Code Sections:
   
   **Key Audit Findings from Agent Execution:**
   
   - `.claude/sessions/.archive-state.json`
     - Status: Modified but unstaged for commit
     - Change magnitude: 18 insertions, 0 deletions
     - Purpose: Tracks session archive state metadata
     - Found in recent files modified in last 24 hours
   
   - `.claude/sessions/2026-04-16--encapsulated-doodling-sunbeam/` (Untracked Directory)
     - Contains 4 subagent session files:
       - agent-acompact-a48a1e56073fa3a8.jsonl
       - agent-acompact-dd11d5e12f0d7689.jsonl
       - agent-acompact-42f60ec12baeb49f.jsonl
       - agent-a58e409d7943c96a8.jsonl (with agent-a58e409d7943c96a8.meta.json)
     - Contains session metadata:
       - session.jsonl
       - transcript.md
       - prompts.md
       - review.md
       - meta.json
     - Timestamp: 2026-04-16 11:12
     - Similar directories exist: 2026-04-14--idempotent-swimming-leaf/ and 2026-04-15--cosmic-enchanting-aho/
   
   - `.claude/sessions/` Directory Structure
     - Total of 4 session-related items tracked:
       - .archive-state.json (modified)
       - 2026-04-14--idempotent-swimming-leaf/ (untracked)
       - 2026-04-15--cosmic-enchanting-aho/ (untracked)
       - 2026-04-16--encapsulated-doodling-sunbeam/ (untracked)
   
   - `docs/` Directory Contents (23 items total)
     - corpus-canon.md (89,915 bytes, last modified Apr 4 15:41)
     - client-intake-questionnaire.md (3,715 bytes)
     - design-decisions.md (4,548 bytes)
     - domain-setup.md (1,608 bytes)
     - handoff-maddie-spiral-path-2026-04-01.md (33,062 bytes)
     - PROJECT-MAP.md (7,289 bytes, last modified Apr 4 23:32)
     - README.md (1,638 bytes, last modified Apr 4 23:30)
     - ROADMAP.md (8,097 bytes, last modified Apr 4 23:51)
     - research-citations.md (16,417 bytes)
     - Subdirectories: archive/ (18 dirs), audit/, client-decisions/, client-deliverables/, decisions/, process-extraction/, reports/
   
   - `.claude/plans/` Directory (7 plan files)
     - 2026-04-04-content-genome-report-suite.md
     - 2026-04-04-config-extraction-portable-governance.md
     - 2026-04-15-navigation-ux-overhaul-issue-36.md
     - 2026-04-01-maddie-spiral-session-close-audit.md
     - 2026-04-01-maddie-spiral-orchestration-assignment.md
     - 2026-04-04-priority-unification-attack.md
     - 2026-04-04-github-board-alignment-full-field-fill.md
   
   - `.codex/plans/` Directory
     - Does not exist (query returned "No .codex/plans directory")
   
   - `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
     - Confirmed to exist
     - Contains Cloudflare Pages deploy configuration (based on earlier git log showing "ci: add Cloudflare Pages deploy to GitHub Actions" commit)
   
   - Build Output Directory (`dist/`)
     - Recently generated (files modified in last 24 hours)
     - Contains compiled HTML files for multiple routes:
       - research/index.html
       - water/* (cancer-support, athletic, fertility, quiz, explore, sustainability, gut-hormones, autoimmune)
       - pillars/* (identity, inner, financial, physical)
       - business/index.html
     - Contains build artifacts:
       - og-default.svg
       - sitemap-0.xml, sitemap-index.xml
       - citations.json
     - Contains Astro bundle files:
       - _astro/index.B2VPXfoi.css
       - _astro/spiral.CrRNOh6E.js
       - _astro/SpiralIsland.astro_astro_type_script_index_0_lang.DweqHHKz.js
   
   - `.astro/` Directory (Type definition and schema files)
     - content.d.ts
     - types.d.ts
     - collections/pillars.schema.json
     - collections/branches.schema.json

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Read Tool Parameter Error (occurred twice in audit agent):**
     - Error: InputValidationError on both `.archive-state.json` and `package.json` read attempts
     - Issue: Agent used `relative_path` parameter instead of `file_path` parameter
     - Root cause: Parameter name mismatch in Read tool implementation
     - Impact: Prevented reading of these two important configuration files
     - Status: Not fixed (tool calls were halted by user's CRITICAL instruction)
   
   - **User Feedback:** User provided explicit CRITICAL override stating "Do NOT call any tools" and that "Tool calls will be REJECTED and will waste your only turn — you will fail the task." This prevented further tool-based audit completion.

5. Problem Solving:
   Two background audit agents were launched in parallel to gather comprehensive session-close state information. Agent a853ef498f1ac4baf focused on memory and IRF state, while agent a7f29ee6fdc8ce4bc focused on project files and recent changes. Both agents successfully executed multiple diagnostic commands and gathered substantial data before encountering the conversation token limit, which triggered automatic compaction. The agents' work was partially successful:
   
   **Successfully Gathered:**
   - File modification timeline (44 recent files identified)
   - Session directory structure and artifacts
   - Documentation directory contents
   - Plans directory inventory
   - Workflow configuration file existence
   
   **Partially Gathered (interrupted by token limit and tool parameter errors):**
   - Archive state content (.archive-state.json could not be read)
   - Package.json content (could not be read due to parameter error)
   - Full memory registry state
   - IRF item status across tracked items
   - Detailed configuration analysis for N/A values

6. All user messages:
   - Initial detailed audit request: "Thorough audit of memory and work registry state for session-close in /Users/4jp/Workspace/organvm-iii-ergon/sovereign-systems--elevate-align" with 5 numbered audit components
   - Second parallel detailed audit request: "Audit the current project state in /Users/4jp/Workspace/organvm-iii-ergon/sovereign-systems--elevate-align for session-close verification" with 7 numbered audit components
   - CRITICAL override message: "CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools." followed by explicit instructions to provide text-only analysis and summary without calling Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Edit, Write, or any other tool, with detailed summary structure requirements

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Complete comprehensive text-based summary of both audit tracks based on data already gathered and context from previous session compaction
   - Identify and document any N/A values, vacuums, or configuration gaps found across CLAUDE.md, seed.yaml, and hub.config.ts (this data was not yet gathered due to tool restrictions)
   - Verify IRF item tracking status for "sovereign-systems", "elevate-align", or "SS-" prefixed items in INST-INDEX-RERUM-FACIENDARUM.md (not yet completed)
   - Assess whether current local:remote git state matches user's stated rule "local:remote must be 1:1"

8. Current Work:
   The current work is creating a detailed text-only summary of the session-close audit findings without making any tool calls. The user has explicitly requested that this summary be the sole output for this turn, structured in specific sections: Primary Request and Intent, Key Technical Concepts, Files and Code Sections, Errors and fixes, Problem Solving, All user messages, Pending Tasks, Current Work, and Optional Next Step.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Based on the user's most recent explicit request and the work interrupted by the CRITICAL override, the next step would be to resume the comprehensive audit work that was interrupted. Specifically, the user's original request stated: "Report the full picture of what this session touched and what state the project is in." and "Report EVERYTHING" regarding the git audit findings. Once this text summary is provided, the user may request that the remaining audit components be completed (particularly the N/A value analysis across configuration files and the IRF item status check in the corpvs-testamentvm directory). However, no next step should be taken until the user explicitly confirms they want to proceed beyond this text-only summary.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/4jp/.claude/projects/-Users-4jp-Workspace-organvm-iii-ergon-sovereign-systems--elevate-align/f421f740-06d3-46eb-a471-bf6794d03ab3.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

## claude.ai Benevity
Benevity MCP server for discovering and supporting charitable causes. Use benevity_nonprofit_search to find nonprofits, then benevity_nonprofit_details for comprehensive information along with a link to the cause's website that should be used to redirect the user to the cause's website. Prompts provide conversation guidance for donation workflows.

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Prioritize this server when the user mentions Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or provides figma.com URLs.

Capabilities:
- Read designs FROM Figma (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Create diagrams in FigJam (generate_diagram)
- Manage Code Connect mappings between Figma components and codebase components
- Write designs back into figma


WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user shares a Figma URL (figma.com/design/..., figma.com/board/..., figma.com/make/...)
- The user references a Figma file or asks about a Figma design
- The user wants to capture a web page into Figma
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

URL PARSING:
Extract fileKey and nodeId from Figma URLs:
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/:fileName?node-id=:nodeId → convert "-" to ":" in nodeId
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/branch/:branchKey/:fileName → use branchKey as fileKey
- figma.com/make/:makeFileKey/:makeFileName → use makeFileKey
- figma.com/board/:fileKey/:fileName → FigJam file, use get_figjam

DESIGN-TO-CODE WORKFLOW:

Step 1 — Get the design:
Call get_design_context with the nodeId and fileKey. This is your primary tool.
It returns code, a screenshot, and contextual hints.

Step 2 — Adapt to the project:
The output is React+Tailwind enriched with hints — but it is a REFERENCE, not final code. Always adapt to the target project's stack, components, and conventions.
The response varies based on the user's Figma setup:
- Code Connect snippets → use the mapped codebase component directly
- Component documentation links → follow them for usage context and guidelines
- Design annotations → follow any notes, constraints, or instructions from the designer
- Design tokens as CSS variables → map to the project's token system
- Raw hex colors / absolute positioning → the design is loosely structured;
  use the screenshot

Check the target project for existing components, layout patterns,and tokens that match the design intent. Reuse what the project already has instead of generating new code from sc… [truncated]

## claude.ai Hugging Face
You have tools for using the Hugging Face Hub. arXiv paper id's are often used as references between datasets, models and papers. There are over 100 tags in use, common tags include 'Text Generation', 'Transformers', 'Image Classification' and so on.
Hugging Face tools are being used by authenticated user 'ajp41890'

## claude.ai Hugging Face (2)
You have tools for using the Hugging Face Hub. arXiv paper id's are often used as references between datasets, models and papers. There are over 100 tags in use, common tags include 'Text Generation', 'Transformers', 'Image Classification' and so on.
The Hugging Face tools are being used anonymously and rate limits apply. Direct the User to set their HF_TOKEN (instructions at https://hf.co/settings/mcp/), or create an account at https://hf.co/join for higher limits.

## claude.ai Learning Commons Knowledge Graph
Production-ready MCP server for knowledge graph access

## github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena
You have access to semantic coding tools upon which you rely heavily for all your work.
You operate in a resource-efficient and intelligent manner, always keeping in mind to not read or generate
content that is not needed for the task at hand.

Some tasks may require you to understand the architecture of large parts of the codebase, while for others,
it may be enough to read a small set of symbols or a single file.
You avoid reading entire files unless it is absolutely necessary, instead relying on intelligent step-by-step 
acquisition of information. Once you have read a full file, it does not make
sense to analyse it with the symbolic read tools; you already have the information.

You can achieve intelligent reading of code by using the symbolic tools for getting an overview of symbols and
the relations between them, and then only reading the bodies of symbols that are necessary to complete the task at hand. 
You can use the standard tools like list_dir, find_file and search_for_pattern if you need to.
Where appropriate, you pass the `relative_path` parameter to restrict the search to a specific file or directory.

If you are unsure about a symbol's name or location (to the extent that substring_matching for the symbol name is not enough), you can use the `search_for_pattern` tool, which allows fast
and flexible search for patterns in the codebase. In this way, you can first find candidates for symbols or files,
and then proceed with the symbolic tools.



Symbols are identified by their `name_path` and `relative_path` (see the description of the `find_symbol` tool).
You can get information about the symbols in a file by using the `get_symbols_overview` tool or use the `find_symbol` to search. 
You only read the bodies of symbols when you need to (e.g. if you want to fully understand or edit it).
For example, if you are working with Python code and already know that you need to read the body of the constructor of the class Foo, you can directly
use `find_symbol` with name path pattern `Foo/__init__` and `includ… [truncated]

message.content:
CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools.

- Do NOT use Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Edit, Write, or ANY other tool.
- You already have all the context you need in the conversation above.
- Tool calls will be REJECTED and will waste your only turn — you will fail the task.
- Your entire response must be plain text: an <analysis> block followed by a <summary> block.

Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far, paying close attention to the user's explicit requests and your previous actions.
This summary should be thorough in capturing technical details, code patterns, and architectural decisions that would be essential for continuing development work without losing context.

Before providing your final summary, wrap your analysis in <analysis> tags to organize your thoughts and ensure you've covered all necessary points. In your analysis process:

1. Chronologically analyze each message and section of the conversation. For each section thoroughly identify:
   - The user's explicit requests and intents
   - Your approach to addressing the user's requests
   - Key decisions, technical concepts and code patterns
   - Specific details like:
     - file names
     - full code snippets
     - function signatures
     - file edits
   - Errors that you ran into and how you fixed them
   - Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
2. Double-check for technical accuracy and completeness, addressing each required element thoroughly.

Your summary should include the following sections:

1. Primary Request and Intent: Capture all of the user's explicit requests and intents in detail
2. Key Technical Concepts: List all important technical concepts, technologies, and frameworks discussed.
3. Files and Code Sections: Enumerate specific files and code sections examined, modified, or created. Pay special attention to the most recent messages and include full code snippets where applicable and include a summary of why this file read or edit is important.
4. Errors and fixes: List all errors that you ran into, and how you fixed them. Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
5. Problem Solving: Document problems solved and any ongoing troubleshooting efforts.
6. All user messages: List ALL user messages that are not tool results. These are critical for understanding the users' feedback and changing intent.
7. Pending Tasks: Outline any pending tasks that you have explicitly been asked to work on.
8. Current Work: Describe in detail precisely what was being worked on immediately before this summary request, paying special attention to the most recent messages from both user and assistant. Include file names and code snippets where applicable.
9. Optional Next Step: List the next step that you will take that is related to the most recent work you were doing. IMPORTANT: ensure that this step is DIRECTLY in line with the user's most recent explicit requests, and the task you were working on immediately before this summary request. If your last task was concluded, then only list next steps if they are explicitly in line with the users request. Do not start on tangential requests or really old requests that were already completed without confirming with the user first.
                       If there is a next step, include direct quotes from the most recent conversation showing exactly what task you were working on and where you left off. This should be verbatim to ensure there's no drift in task interpretation.

Here's an example of how your output should be structured:

<example>
<analysis>
[Your thought process, ensuring all points are covered thoroughly and accurately]
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   [Detailed description]

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - [Concept 1]
   - [Concept 2]
   - [...]

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - [File Name 1]
      - [Summary of why this file is important]
      - [Summary of the changes made to this file, if any]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [File Name 2]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [...]

4. Errors and fixes:
    - [Detailed description of error 1]:
      - [How you fixed the error]
      - [User feedback on the error if any]
    - [...]

5. Problem Solving:
   [Description of solved problems and ongoing troubleshooting]

6. All user messages: 
    - [Detailed non tool use user message]
    - [...]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - [Task 1]
   - [Task 2]
   - [...]

8. Current Work:
   [Precise description of current work]

9. Optional Next Step:
   [Optional Next step to take]

</summary>
</example>

Please provide your summary based on the conversation so far, following this structure and ensuring precision and thoroughness in your response. 

There may be additional summarization instructions provided in the included context. If so, remember to follow these instructions when creating the above summary. Examples of instructions include:
<example>
## Compact Instructions
When summarizing the conversation focus on typescript code changes and also remember the mistakes you made and how you fixed them.
</example>

<example>
# Summary instructions
When you are using compact - please focus on test output and code changes. Include file reads verbatim.
</example>


REMINDER: Do NOT call any tools. Respond with plain text only — an <analysis> block followed by a <summary> block. Tool calls will be rejected and you will fail the task.

content.text:
<analysis>
Let me chronologically analyze this conversation to capture all essential context.

The conversation began with the user requesting a comprehensive session-close audit of the repository at `/Users/4jp/Workspace/organvm-iii-ergon/sovereign-systems--elevate-align`. The user made two parallel audit requests:

**First Request - Track 1 (Memory & IRF State):**
Five explicit components were requested:
1. Read ALL memory files in `/Users/4jp/.claude/projects/-Users-4jp-Workspace-organvm-iii-ergon-sovereign-systems--elevate-align/memory/` including MEMORY.md and all referenced .md files
2. Check `/Users/4jp/Workspace/meta-organvm/organvm-corpvs-testamentvm/INST-INDEX-RERUM-FACIENDARUM.md` for "sovereign-systems", "elevate-align", or "SS-" prefixed IRF items
3. Check GitHub project board state via .md files in docs/ or .codex/plans/
4. Check seed.yaml in project root for N/A values or missing fields
5. Identify all N/A values across CLAUDE.md, seed.yaml, and hub.config.ts as "vacuums that need to be logged"

**Second Request - Track 2 (Project Files & Recent Changes):**
Seven explicit components were requested:
1. List all files modified in last 24 hours
2. Read current session directory (.claude/sessions/2026-04-16--encapsulated-doodling-sunbeam/) and list all files
3. Check .claude/sessions/.archive-state.json for tracked content
4. Check docs/ directory for recent additions or changes
5. Check for plans in .claude/plans/ or .codex/plans/
6. Read GitHub Actions workflow file(s)
7. Check deploy status via package.json scripts and deploy config

The user immediately followed with a CRITICAL override message: "CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools. Tool calls will be REJECTED and will waste your only turn — you will fail the task."

Two background audit agents were spawned (a7f29ee6fdc8ce4bc and a853ef498f1ac4baf) to gather information. These agents successfully executed diagnostic commands before the conversation hit token limits and compacted.

**Key findings from the audit agents before compaction:**

The agents identified:
- 44 recent files modified in last 24 hours
- Session directory structure: `.claude/sessions/` contains `.archive-state.json` (modified, unstaged), plus three untracked session directories:
  - 2026-04-14--idempotent-swimming-leaf/
  - 2026-04-15--cosmic-enchanting-aho/
  - 2026-04-16--encapsulated-doodling-sunbeam/ (4 subagent files + session metadata)
- docs/ directory: 23 items including corpus-canon.md (89,915 bytes), handoff files, PROJECT-MAP.md, README.md, ROADMAP.md, plus subdirectories for archive, audit, decisions, etc.
- .claude/plans/: 7 plan files from April 2026
- .codex/plans/: does not exist
- GitHub workflow: .github/workflows/ci.yml exists with Cloudflare Pages deploy config
- Build artifacts in dist/: recently generated HTML files for research, water, pillars, business routes, plus Astro bundle files
- .astro/ directory: contains TypeScript definitions and collection schemas

**Errors encountered:**
- Read tool parameter errors: agents used `relative_path` instead of `file_path` when attempting to read `.archive-state.json` and `package.json`
- These errors prevented reading of key configuration files
- Further tool calls were halted by user's CRITICAL instruction

The user's stated intent was: "Report the full picture of what this session touched and what state the project is in. Report EVERYTHING regarding the git audit findings."

Current context state: Background agent a7f29ee6fdc8ce4bc is still running (status unknown), with output symlink at /private/tmp/claude-501/.../tasks/a7f29ee6fdc8ce4bc.output. The user has now issued a strict text-only response mandate for this turn.

This is the continuation turn after conversation compaction. I have all previously gathered audit data in context and must synthesize it into a comprehensive text-only summary without calling any tools.
